title: Higher Dimensional Fourier Quasicrystals from Lee-Yang Varieties
speaker: Pavel Kurasov
abstract:
Fourier Quasicrystals (FQ) are defined as crystalline measures
μ=∑λ∈Λaλδλ,μ̂ =∑s∈Sbsδs, so that not only μ (and hence μ̂ ) are tempered distributions, but also |μ|:=∑λ∈Λ|aλ|δλand|μ̂ |:=∑s∈S|bs|δs,
are tempered. 
One-dimensional FQs with positive integer weights (that is aλ∈ℕ) can be described using stable Lee-Yang polynomials, as was proven in a joint work with Peter Sarnak. Multidimensional Fourier quasicrystals are discussed in the current talk. It is shown that a rather general family of FQs in ℝd
 with positive integer weights can be constructed using co-dimension d Lee-Yang varieties in ℂn,n greater than d.
 These complex algebraic varieties are symmetric and avoid certain regions in ℂn, thus generalising zero sets of Lee-Yang polynomials. 
It is shown that such FQs can be supported by Delaunay almost periodic sets and are genuinely multidimensional in the sense that their restriction to any one-dimensional subspace is not given by a one-dimensional FQ. Connections to alternative recent approaches by Yves Meyer, Lawton-Tsikh and de Courcy-Ireland-K. are clarified. Is it possible that our construction gives all multidimensional FQs with positive integer masses?
This is joint work with L. Alon, M. Kummer, and C. Vinzant (arXiv:2407.11184).

proof_stages.label: Fourier-quasicrystal problem and one-dimensional template
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Delone sets, geometric quasicrystals, crystalline measures, and Fourier quasicrystals, then reviews the earlier one-dimensional Lee-Yang/stable-polynomial construction and its classification results as the template for the higher-dimensional problem.

proof_stages.label: Strict Lee-Yang varieties and the candidate set
proof_stages.summary: The speaker replaces a Lee-Yang polynomial by a codimension-d strict Lee-Yang variety satisfying involution, high sign-variation, and smooth torus-intersection hypotheses, chooses a positive-minor matrix L, and defines the candidate set Lambda by exp(iLw) lying on the variety.

proof_stages.label: Reality via variation and the positive Grassmannian
proof_stages.summary: Using the Gantmacher-Krein variation-diminishing property for positive Grassmannian subspaces, the imaginary part of any solution is forced to vanish, so the candidate set Lambda is actually real.

proof_stages.label: Transversality and Delone bounds
proof_stages.summary: The variety is passed to a periodic real torus model, transversality with the positive Grassmannian planes is obtained from complementary variation bounds, and shifted intersection sets Lambda_y plus nearest- and second-nearest-point functions give relative density and uniform discreteness.

proof_stages.label: Lee-Yang measures, projections, and Fourier support
proof_stages.summary: A natural measure on the torus intersection is constructed as a small-tube limit, decomposed into a positive linear combination of basic projection measures with multidegrees, and shown to have Fourier coefficients supported only in the small-variation region.

proof_stages.label: Summation formula and Fourier-quasicrystal conclusion
proof_stages.summary: Periodic approximants and Fourier series are used to pass to a summation formula for Lambda, after which terms with the same frequency are regrouped to obtain a discrete tempered spectrum and hence a Fourier quasicrystal.

proof_stages.label: Nontriviality, arithmetic restrictions, and comparison
proof_stages.summary: The final part records almost-periodicity and arithmetic restrictions, explains the conditional status of proving non-product nontriviality via a Shapiro-type conjecture for trigonometric polynomials, and compares the construction with other multidimensional approaches.

examples.name: Lattice Dirac comb / ideal crystal
examples.note: The full-rank lattice with a finite motif and its Dirac-comb Poisson summation formula illustrate the trivial periodic model against which Fourier quasicrystals are compared.
examples.engagement_reason: the ideal lattice Dirac comb is introduced as the standard crystal example and its Poisson formula is stated without deriving or computing it

examples.name: Irrational cut-and-project set
examples.note: A lattice strip projected to an irrational line makes the standard geometric quasicrystal definition concrete and motivates why a denser Fourier-side spectrum is unsatisfactory.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs through the irrational cut-and-project construction with a lattice, strip, projection, and reasons that irrationality gives nonperiodicity and a dense Fourier-side set

passes.reason: An irrational cut-and-project set is a standard aperiodic model set, used here to show the projected dual spectrum can be dense rather than discrete.
an irrational cut-and-project set is a standard aperiodic quasicrystal used to show the naive Fourier spectrum becomes dense rather than discrete
